Q1: I tried to set up a pretty structured routine, honestly putting in around 32 hours a week into studying. I'd block out time in the mornings and evenings, usually working through problems hands-on or doing practice exercises since that's how I learn best. I had good intentions with scheduling, but I'll admit my actual attendance to live sessions was basically nonexistent because I found I could learn better working through materials on my own terms. The structure was there on paper, but I adapted it constantly based on what felt productive.
Q2: My pattern definitely shifted based on what was due and what I had going on with my extracurriculars. I'd have these intense weeks where I'd be grinding through content, then lighter weeks where I focused more on other commitments. Deadlines were the main driver of when I'd really buckle down, but I tried to keep some consistency with at least logging in and checking what needed attention. It wasn't perfectly regular, but it wasn't chaotic either.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was something specific I needed to check—like assignment updates, new materials posted, or to see discussion threads. Sometimes it was just habit, like a quick check while having coffee to see if anything changed. I also logged in a lot to download resources so I could work with them offline later. The notifications would prompt me too, but honestly, boredom sometimes just made me scroll through without much purpose.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS became pretty automatic after the first few weeks. I'd wake up, grab my phone or laptop, and just open it like checking social media. It became part of my morning routine especially. Having extracurriculars meant I needed to stay organized, so making LMS checks habitual helped me not miss important deadlines even when I wasn't fully mentally present.
Q5: The video content was actually pretty useful—I watched a decent amount of it because I could pause, rewind, and work through concepts at my own pace. The discussion forums were helpful too when peers explained things differently than the instructor. I also appreciated any hands-on exercises or simulations since I'm a kinesthetic learner. Anything I could actively engage with rather than just read passively felt more valuable to me.
Q6: I definitely skipped over a lot of the reading-heavy PDFs and supplementary articles. They just didn't hold my attention, and I'd find myself zoning out. Some of the optional quizzes I ignored because they felt repetitive. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I'd often push it aside thinking I'd get to it later, which usually meant never.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to what I'd be tested on or if it helped me actually understand something I was struggling with. Practical application was huge—if I could do something with my hands or work through a problem step by step, I was in. Pure reading or passive watching without interaction felt like a waste when I had limited energy to give.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like they were competing for time I didn't have. Required ones got priority because there were consequences for skipping them. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that looks interesting' but then never actually do it because something else always seemed more urgent. The pressure of requirements made those feel more legitimate somehow, even if the optional ones might have been more enriching.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded tons of materials and worked through them on my tablet or printed them out so I could annotate by hand. I used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts that weren't clicking. I also had a study group that met in person where we'd work through problems together on whiteboards. A lot of my actual learning happened away from any screen the LMS could track.
Q10: There were whole study sessions where I'd have my notes spread out on my desk, working through practice problems from a textbook I bought separately. Or I'd be in the library with my study group for hours, none of which showed up anywhere online. The mindfulness exercises I did to manage stress also helped me focus better when I did study, but that's invisible to any system tracking my clicks.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or PDF once, then spend days working through it offline. I preferred having materials locally so I didn't need internet or could study during commutes. The LMS would show one quick access, but I might have reviewed that same material five or six times on my own device afterward.
Q12: Mid-semester hit hard when multiple courses piled up at once. I started feeling bored with the content because I wasn't seeing progress despite putting in hours. The low exam score was demoralizing even though I thought I understood the material. My extracurriculars also ramped up during that period, so I was spread thin and just going through the motions sometimes.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued from everything else going on, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at it without really processing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely for a while. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage constantly, and sometimes the course lost out to things that felt more immediately pressing.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing while doing other things, or I'd scroll through readings without really absorbing them. I was technically 'active' but mentally checked out. It's like my body was going through the motions but my brain was elsewhere. Those sessions felt hollow afterward, like I'd wasted time pretending to study.
Q15: I postponed the tougher assignments because they felt overwhelming, even though I knew they were important. There was this weird avoidance thing where the more I knew something mattered, the more anxious it made me to start, so I'd do easier tasks first. I'd tell myself I'd get to it tomorrow when I was fresher, but tomorrow kept getting pushed back.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become hyper-focused, cramming in work I should have spread out over weeks. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically in the 48 hours before anything was due. Without deadlines, I'd drift and lose urgency. They were stressful but honestly necessary for me to function.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially if it was constructive, I'd re-engage with the material to understand where I went wrong. But feedback was sometimes slow or vague, which made it hard to adjust. Good feedback motivated me to try harder; absent or harsh feedback made me want to disengage. It really shaped whether I felt like the effort was worth it.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad. Good because I could work at my own pace, bad because without mandatory attendance or check-ins, I let things slide. Having 16 online courses meant I was juggling a lot, and this one sometimes got deprioritized because the structure didn't force me to show up consistently.
Q19: The hands-on components and interactive elements made engagement easier—those matched how I learn. But the heavy reliance on readings and passive content made it harder. Also, the sheer volume of material across all my courses made it difficult to give this one the attention it probably needed. Clearer pathways through the content would have helped me stay on track.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to figure things out but struggling to produce quality work. They might be exploring resources, rewatching videos, reading discussions—all high activity—but not feeling confident enough to submit. Or they're dealing with stuff outside academics that's eating into their capacity to actually complete things. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show in the logs. Someone could download everything once, study intensively from printed notes, and ace the exam without ever logging in again. The LMS only captures a fraction of the learning process. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of that time entirely. They can't tell if you're focused or distracted, if you understood something or just scrolled past it. They definitely miss offline study, group work, and the mental effort that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, wrestling with concepts, wanting to understand rather than just complete. It's when I'm curious and invested, not just checking boxes. True engagement is active—it's asking questions, making connections, caring about whether I actually learn something. Clicking through content isn't engagement; it's just motion.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my study group sessions, the hours I spent with physical notes, or the mindfulness practice that helped me manage stress and stay resilient. It wouldn't capture the conversations with peers outside the platform or the moments of genuine insight that happened while walking or thinking. My engagement was real, just not always digital.
